title: Sperm whales may make their own vowel sounds, similar to human language
description: Sperm whales, which make clicking sounds to communicate, use different “vowels” in ways similar to human speech.

title: Wild Resonance #1: Animal syntax in the trees and seas: A conversation with Dr. Mason Youngblood
description: Dr. Youngblood and Stephen explore Mason's fascinating research on vocal efficiency in house finches and cetaceans.

title: More than a billion birds could be dying from flying into buildings each year in the U.S.
description: We are losing them even if we don’t see them.

title: All languages follow this pattern: So does humpback whale song
description: The animals’ complex songs share structural patterns with human language that may make them easier for whales to learn.

title: Whale songs obey basic rules of human languages
description: For all the world’s linguistic diversity, human languages still obey some universal patterns—and humpback whale song follows many of the same rules.

title: What the science of baby-speak can tell us about whale songs
description: Just like popular songs on TikTok, new humpback whale songs can rapidly spread across regions and populations to replace older melodies.

title: Humpback whale songs have patterns that resemble human language
description: Humpback whale songs have statistical patterns in their structure that are remarkably similar to those seen in human language.

title: Whalesong patterns follow a universal law of human language: New research finds
description: Techniques inspired by how human infants learn language reveal that whale song shares statistically coherent parts and Zipfian frequency distributions with human speech.

title: Canada's sparrows are singing a new song: You'll hear it soon
description: Over 20 years, scientists tracked the transformation of the traditional trill of a common bird from western Canada to Ontario.
